Tafseer:
1. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(By those [winds] that scatter scattering, and those [clouds] that carry a load, and those [ships] that glide with ease, and those [angels] who apportion a matter) (1-4)
'Wa' is for an oath, and the oath is for testimony:
In 'Wa-dh-dhariyati', the 'wa' is for an oath, and we repeatedly explain in this book that the oaths sworn by things in the Qur'an are not intended to glorify these things, but to present them as testimony for the call mentioned after the oath or understood from the context. Thus, this oath is also for testimony. If it is translated with the word 'testimony', we believe it will be more meaningful.
Dhariyat:
'Dhariyat' refers to the winds that scatter dust. This word is used as an attribute of the winds. However, it has become so well known for its noun that it is used in its place. The addition of the word 'dharwa' after 'dhariyati' adds an emphasis to the verb, in the same way as 'darba darban' creates the concept of emphasizing the verb. Conveying the meaning of such emphasis in translation is sometimes difficult. Here, if the word 'violent/turbulent' is added to the winds, we believe this meaning will be conveyed.
Benefits of connection with 'fa':
'fa-l-hamilati wiqran': When attributes are connected with 'fa', it serves as proof of two things. First, there is a sequential order among them. Second, all these attributes belong to the same noun. According to this rule of Arabic grammar, the three attributes mentioned here with 'fa' must belong to the winds. The opinion of those who regarded them as attributes of different things is contrary to Arabic grammar as well as Qur'an's precedents.
It is stated in Surah Al-Adiyat:
(By those [horses] that run panting, and those that strike sparks [with their hooves], and those that make raids in the morning, and stir up thereby dust, and penetrate thereby into the center collectively) (1-5)
Obviously, all these attributes do not belong to different things, but to horses, and on reflection, it is clear that sequential order is also maintained in their description.
Examples of this style are numerous in classical Arabic. We present only a famous verse by Ibn Ziyabah:
(Alas, from Ziyabah, for Haris who raided in the morning, then plundered, then departed)
Wiqr:
'Wiqr' means load or burden. Though it can refer to any load or burden that the winds carry—such as dust, pebbles, etc.—its recognized usage is for clouds. For example:
(And He is the One Who sends the winds as good tidings before His mercy, until, when they have carried heavy clouds, We drive them to a dead land and We send down rain thereby) (Al-A'raf, 7:57).
The meaning of 'jariyat' and clearing a misunderstanding:
'fa-l-jariyati yusran': This attribute also belongs to the winds. The opinion of those who interpreted it as ships is contrary to the rule we mentioned above. 'Yusr' means slowly and gently. The general rule is that first, violent and dust-raising winds blow, which drive the clouds from different directions and gather them layer upon layer over the region decreed by Allah Almighty to be watered. Then, the speed of the wind becomes gentle, and rain begins to fall.
The meaning of apportioning a matter:
'fa-l-muqassimati amran': 'Qasama-l-amr' means delivering what was decreed or destined for someone. That is: after carrying the clouds, these winds apportion the matter in accordance with the command of their Lord—meaning they pour down rain in accordance with the amount decreed for a region, making some areas completely wet, leaving some partially thirsty, and some dry. And if Allah Almighty commands them, they descend on some regions as storms and floods, devastating the entire area. The variations in the winds' actions are highly amazing—dealing with one nation in one way and with another in another way; serving as glad tidings of mercy for one, and as a storm of punishment for another, as detailed further on, if Allah wills.
