(Indeed, what you are promised is true, and indeed, the Recompense is to occur) (5-6)
The subject of the oath:
This is the subject of the oath (muqsam 'alayh). That is: these amazing actions of the winds, which you constantly witness, are witnesses that what you are warned of is completely true, and indeed, the Recompense is bound to occur.
The meaning of 'to'adoon':
In 'Innama to'adoon', the word is derived from 'wa'd' (promise), which encompasses all things promised through the prophets—namely: the Resurrection, gathering, recompense, mercy, and wrath. The author of Al-Kashshaf regards 'And indeed, the Recompense is to occur' as a specific mention connected to a general one. However, my inclination is that 'to'adoon' is derived from 'wa'eed' (warning), referring to the punishment that inevitably befell those who rejected the messengers. Thus, the oath by the winds' amazing actions is sworn in my view to prove two things: first, that the punishment of which the Quraysh are warned—which they regard as a mere threat—is indeed a true warning; and second, the Recompense is bound to occur, which they regard as highly unlikely.
Several arguments support my view, but there is no need for details. Pointing to three matters is sufficient:
First, this defines the areas of both parts of the subject of the oath easily.
Second, Allah's messengers warned their nations of two punishments: first, the punishment in this world if they persisted in their denial; second, the punishment of the Hereafter if they died in disbelief. Both punishments are mentioned in the Qur'an, and we explain them. This requires that both are mentioned here, since the oath is a witness to both. This will be clarified.
Third, further on in this Surah, reference is made to some nations who rejected the messengers, as a confirmation of this warning. There you will see that the winds' actions played a primary role in their destruction.
A grammatical point:
Note that the warning of the worldly punishment is expressed through a verb, while the Recompense of the Hereafter is expressed through a noun. The reason is clear: the worldly punishment is a temporary event subject to conditions and circumstances; but the law of Recompense is the purpose and inevitable result of creation, which is why the first is expressed through a verb, and the second through a noun.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 5 and 6 was combined in the original text.]

(And [by] the heaven full of pathways) (7)
Research on 'dhat-il-hubuk':
'Sama'' can refer to the heaven as well as the clouds, as used in both meanings in the Qur'an. However, if the heaven is meant, it must be interpreted along with the attribute 'dhat-il-hubuk'. Therefore, what needs research is this attribute. Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) explained this word in classical Arabic. We present the essential part of his explanation:
'"Hubuk" means binding and knotting. From this, it was used for the strength and stability created in the structure of something. From this is "hibak", whose plural is "hubuk". "Hubuk" refers to the stripes, rifts, and waves created in a firmly woven fabric... Al-Farra' explained that "hubuk" refers to the waves and ripples created in sand or water when the wind blows over it. From this, it was used to describe the clouds, because the clouds appear in the heaven like waves and layer upon layer of cotton wool. Imru' al-Qays, praising elevated palaces covered in clouds, wrote:
"Red-striped palaces covered in clouds [dhat-il-hubuk] as if they were striped sheets"
This describes winter clouds, presenting a correct picture of their color and layers...
In our view, those who interpreted "dhat-il-hubuk" as the orbits of stars—whether because of their strength or because stars are fixed in them—are incorrect.
...This word is used in the meaning of stripes, rifts, waves, and lines.'
In the light of our teacher's research, this oath is by the red-striped winter clouds that appear with the violent northern wind. Thus, after the oath by the winds, this oath by the clouds completes it, because there is an interconnected relationship between the winds and clouds. This addition points specifically to the destructive aspect of the winds.

(Indeed, you are in differing speech) (8)
Rebuking the opponents:
This is not the response (jawab) to the oath, but rebukes the opponents. The response appeared above, and since this second oath completes the first, there was no need to repeat the response; instead, the deniers are rebuked for being in differing speech and contradiction of thought, showing that with these testimonies present, there is no room to deny the warning of punishment or doubt the Recompense.
Examples of omitting the response to an oath when it is clear exist in the Qur'an. A clear example appeared in Surah Qaf:
(Qaf. By the honored Qur'an [it is the word of Allah]. But they wonder that there has come to them a warner from among themselves, and the disbelievers say, 'This is an amazing thing.') (Qaf, 50:1-2).
In this verse, the response is omitted; instead, the opponents are rebuked for their defiance. This same style was adopted in Surah Al-Buruj:
(By the heaven containing constellations, and [by] the promised Day, and [by] the witness and the witnessed, destroyed were the companions of the trench) (Al-Buruj, 85:1-4).
The contradiction of the opponents' thought:
'Differing speech' (qawlim-mukhtalif) points to their contradiction of thought and speech regarding the Resurrection. We have repeatedly explained that not all polytheists of the Arabs were open deniers; rather, alongside the deniers, there was a group of doubters who regarded it as unlikely. Furthermore, a large group assumed their partners and intercessors would save them from God's grasp. They accepted His attributes that require Recompense, yet denied or doubted the Recompense itself. This contradiction of thought is pointed to here, showing that what the Qur'an is informing them of is an obvious truth, provided they do not introduce unnatural obstacles. Refer also to 'they are in a confused condition' in the previous Surah.

(Deluded away from it is he who is deluded) (9)
This is an independent sentence, not an attribute of 'differing speech'. The meaning is: if they reflect free of contradiction, the Recompense is an obvious truth, but those whose intellects are turned away are deluded. 'Afak' means turning away, and 'mafook' is one whose intellect has been turned away. This points to the divine law explained in: (So when they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate) (As-Saff, 61:5) and other similar verses—meaning: they did not use their intellects; therefore, their intellects were turned away, leaving them unable to see what the entire universe bears witness to.

(Destroyed are the conjecturers, who are within a flood of confusion, heedless) (10-11)
The ruin of those who rely on conjecture:
This is also a sentence of rebuke, showing that they rely on conjecture and have abandoned their intellects; therefore, they do not understand the proofs of the universe. Having lost insight, they rely entirely on conjecture, and on its strength, they deny the clear truth that is contrary to their desires, even though conjecture cannot replace the truth. One Day, they will realize that their reliance on conjecture was the cause of their ruin.
What is important is arranged with care:
'Kharas' means guessing and estimating. 'Kharasa-n-nakhla' means: he estimated the fruits of the palm tree. 'Kharasa fi-l-hadith' means: he made a baseless statement regarding something without reflecting on it.
The more important and far-reaching an issue is for human life, the more care Allah Almighty has spent on explaining it. He left only those minor matters to conjecture which have no significant consequence. The primary matters on which human success depends are not left to conjecture; rather, He established His proof fully so that no excuse remains. Resorting to conjecture in such matters is like a person turning off the light Allah has provided and closing his eyes to find the path in a dark night.
The issue of the Hereafter is most important; therefore, He has not left any aspect of it unclear, but has established signs in nature to guide. He gave eyes to see and intellect to understand, provided man uses them. Furthermore, He endowed human nature with the inclinations necessary to remain alert and choose the right path. Most importantly, He revealed His scriptures and sent His messengers to clarify the straight path and its required provision.
If a person ignores all of this to choose his own path of conjecture, it means he loathes the light and prefers to wander in darkness.
The cause of relying on conjecture:
'who are within a flood of confusion, heedless' - This describes their character, showing why they chose conjecture: they are covered in the darkness of desires and are in such heedlessness that they never try to emerge from it. 'Ghamrah' refers to the darkness of desires and worldly pursuits. 'Sahoon' is a second predicate, showing their constant heedlessness—meaning they never try to emerge from it. If someone tries to warn them, it seems heavy on their hearts, and they seek false support to reassure themselves.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 10 and 11 was combined in the original text.]

(They ask, 'When is the Day of Recompense?') (12)
The objection of the deniers of the Recompense:
To silence the warners, they ask: 'Where is the Day of Recompense? When will it occur!' This question contains denial, mockery, and haste. The meaning is: if such a Day is to occur, why does it not arrive! We are tired of these warnings, but it has not arrived. This is a mere threat to intimidate us. If it is a reality, bring it; otherwise, we will not believe!
Recording this question is intended to show that people hide behind such excuses to flee from realities, even though they know that if the Recompense is proved by the signs of nature and is a requirement of His attributes, it cannot be denied merely because its exact time cannot be stated. Such an objection is equivalent to denying a reality through conjecture; therefore, the Qur'an described them as 'conjecturers'.

([It is] the Day they will be tried over the Fire) (13)
Answering in accordance with their mindset:
Since this question was asked for denial and mockery, the Qur'an answered in accordance with their mindset. Note that they knew that its exact time is known only to Allah, and His not disclosing it does not affect the reality. Therefore, the Qur'an, instead of stating its time, portrayed the situation they will face: this Day of Recompense will occur when they will be tried over the Fire. The meaning is: they may mock, but this is the end they will face.
The word 'fatn' (trial) was explained: it means burning and heating, and also testing. Here, 'yuftanoon' points to both meanings. First, they will be burned; second, the Fire they will be burned over is the very desires and worldly goods they were tried with in the world, and in whose love they ignored the Recompense. This will be clarified.

('Taste your trial! This is what you were impatient for!') (14)
'Fitnah' (trial) here is attributed to its object—meaning: the worldly goods that became a trial for you, and in whose love you turned away from the Hereafter, have appeared in their actual form; so taste their consequence! This is what you were hastening.

3. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (15-19)
Above, the mention of those who lived a completely heedless and careless life regarding recompense was presented. If anyone tried to awaken them from this sleep of negligence, they tried to silence him with the response: 'If there is a Day of Recompense, where is it? Bring it and show it to us.' Now, in contrast to them, the end of those who regarded this Day as a reality, feared it, and remained busy in preparing for it is described. Read the verses.
4. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(Indeed, the righteous will be among gardens and springs) (15)
The essence of Taqwa:
'Muttaqeen' (the righteous/those who fear God) is a comprehensive attribute used in the Qur'an for those who live within the limits and boundaries prescribed by God. While it primarily means this, here—in contrast to the preceding verses describing those who lived heedless of the Hereafter—this attribute specifically highlights the aspect of fearing the Recompense. That is: it refers to those who took every step in their lives keeping in view that they must account for their every word and action, and face punishment for every transgression. Indeed, this very fear of the Recompense is the true essence of Taqwa. The Taqwa that lacks this spirit is merely a formal and commercial Taqwa, which has no value before God. Regarding these people, it is stated: indeed, they will be among gardens and springs. While the end of those who chose worldly pleasures over the Hereafter was described as being burned over the Fire of those very pleasures, being told: 'Taste the consequence of your pleasures,' these people, having given no value to worldly pleasures in comparison to the Hereafter, will be admitted by Allah into gardens and springs. 'Gardens' (jannat) and 'springs' (uyoon) are interconnected, serving as a comprehensive description of the blessings of the Hereafter. 'Fi' (in/among) is a proof that they will be completely surrounded by these blessings. They will have blessings all around them.

(Accepting what their Lord has given them. Indeed, they were before that doers of good) (16)
Those who respected God's restrictions in the world will have freedom in the Hereafter:
This portrays their free enjoyment of these blessings. 'Akhidheen' (accepting) is grammatically a circumstantial clause (hal), portraying the situation: they will be receiving whatever their Lord has given them. The past tense in 'what He has given them' (ma atahum) points to the reality that the decision and promise of these gifts have already been finalized by their Lord; they will not have to wait for any new decision. It will be the period of enjoying these blessings. They will take whatever they desire from His non-perishing treasuries, as much as they want and whenever they want. No restriction will lie on them. They respected the restrictions He placed in the world; as a reward, Allah Almighty will bless them with these things and leave them free: 'Enjoy them as you wish, no restriction remains on you.'
The spirit of Ihsan within Taqwa:
'Indeed, they were before that doers of good' (innahum kanu qabla dhalika muhsineen) - This states the cause of His immense favor: they lived in this world as 'muhsineen' (doers of good). We have translated 'muhsineen' in various places as 'doers of good', referring to those who execute every command of their Lord in the manner it deserves to be executed. This quality is produced only in those in whom belief in the Recompense is firmly established. Those who possess this firm belief perform every deed as if they see God, because they know that even if they do not see Him, He sees them.
Describing the righteous as 'muhsineen' throws light on their inner self—showing that because they believed in the Recompense, their Taqwa was not merely formal, but contained the spirit of Ihsan. We noted that only this Taqwa has value before Allah, and is produced by firm belief in the Recompense.

(They used to sleep but little of the night) (17)
Some signs of Taqwa and Ihsan:
This points to the signs of their Taqwa and Ihsan: they slept little during the night. That is: they did not spend their nights in beds of luxury in heedlessness like the careless, but spent them in preparing for the Day of Account. Most part of their night was spent in prostration, standing, remembrance, and reflection before God.
This is described as the inevitable effect of believing in the Hereafter. Those who are anxious for the Hereafter do not sleep a sleep of heedlessness. They are constantly anxious that this might be the final night of their lives; therefore, their sleep is a light sleep. They rise during the night to remember their Lord and ask for His forgiveness. The state of such people is described elsewhere as:
(Their sides depart from their beds; they supplicate their Lord in fear and hope, and from what We have provided them, they spend) (As-Sajdah, 32:16).
While this sentence can be structured grammatically in various ways, the meaning remains the same. One structure is: 'indeed, their sleeping during the night was little' (innahum kanu qaleelan hujoo'uhum); another is: 'they used to sleep little during the night' (kanu yahja'oona qaleelan minal-layl). Regardless of the grammar, there is no significant difference in meaning. Some have interpreted it otherwise, but their view is contrary to Arabic grammar as well as Qur'anic precedents; hence, there is no need to discuss it.
'Hujo' ' means sleeping, and this verse clearly shows that the specific sign of the righteous who are of the rank of Ihsan is that they sleep little during the night, spending its major part in remembrance, reflection, and seeking forgiveness. This is also supported by the Qur'an—for example: (O you wrapped in garments, arise [to pray] the night, except for a little) (Al-Muzzammil, 73:1-2), followed by: (its half or subtract from it a little or add to it) (73:3-4).
This shows that night vigil is a specific sign of the righteous who are of the rank of Ihsan, and those who desire this rank must prioritize its commitment. As for those who lack the courage to break through these difficult passes, there are some concessions for them, which will be explained in Surah Al-Muzzammil.

(And in the hours before dawn they would ask forgiveness) (18)
The hours before dawn (sahar) refer to the time shortly before the breaking of dawn. This states the ultimate purpose of their night vigil, prostrations, and standing: their final act during the night is that in the hours before dawn, they pray for His forgiveness, asking Him to overlook their sins and protect them within His mercy on the Day of Recompense. This reveals that they did not expect their night vigil and prostrations to earn them any high status, nor did they demand it; rather, their only desire was that Allah Almighty overlook their shortcomings and forgive them; hence, their night of worship concluded with seeking forgiveness.
The actual purpose of worship in Islam:
This reveals that the purpose of worship and spiritual exercise in Islam is completely different from other religions. In other religions, the purpose of worship is attaining visions, witnessing, union with God, and other such things. The yogis, ascetics, and monks perform their exercises to attain these. But in Islam, the primary purpose is seeking His forgiveness and pleasure. If any other objective is made the primary purpose of worship, it has no value in Islam. Influenced by Hindu philosophy, some of our Sufis made these very things their objective, which led their spiritual training in a completely different direction. We explained some aspects of this in our book 'Purification of the Soul'.
The most conducive hour for seeking forgiveness:
The most conducive hour is the latter part of the night and the time of sahar. At this hour, as established from the famous Hadith Qudsi, Allah's mercy is in anticipation of those who seek forgiveness. But the courage to benefit from this hour belongs only to the sincere seekers. Not everyone can spend his night in prostration and standing, and then present himself as a beggar for forgiveness at his Lord's door. The courage shown by those who do so is the guarantee of their sincerity; hence, Allah's mercy turns toward them. The true attractor of His mercy is the servant's sincerity; and when sincerity is present, there is no lack of His favor.

(And in their wealth was a right for the petitioner and the deprived) (19)
The righteous recognize the rights of God's creation just as they recognize the rights of God:
That is: these righteous, just as they recognized the rights of God, fulfilled the rights of His creation. They did not regard their wealth as belonging solely to themselves, but believed that those who asked and those who were deprived had a known right in it, which they fulfilled just as any right is fulfilled. That is, they did not fall into the delusion that their wealth belonged solely to them, but realized that what He bestowed beyond their needs was a trust of others, which must be delivered to its recipients; otherwise, it would be a betrayal (khiyanah) that is subject to His questioning.
Who is meant by 'the deprived' (Al-Mahroom)?
It refers specifically to those who once possessed wealth but fell into need due to some turn of fortune. In the Qur'an, they are described as 'al-gharimeen' and included among the recipients of zakat. The deprived also include those needy individuals who face hunger but refuse to suffer the disgrace of begging. Particularly, those who were once wealthy possess a self-respect that prevents them from begging. Since this word appears here in contrast to 'the petitioner' (sa'il), context indicates that it refers to those needy who do not beg. Preserving their self-respect is a great virtue. Elsewhere, the believers are instructed to search out such needy individuals rather than waiting for them to beg at their doors. In Surah Al-Baqarah, they are described as:
(For the needy who have been restricted in the cause of Allah, unable to move about in the land. An ignorant man would think them wealthy because of their self-restraint. You will know them by their mark. They do not beg of people at all.) (Al-Baqarah, 2:273).

5. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (20-23)
In the subsequent verses, the primary theme of the Surah—the Resurrection and recompense—is resumed. While the preceding verses argued from the actions of the winds and clouds, these verses direct us to open our eyes to the signs of nature and the human soul, and believe in their conclusions. It is stated:
6. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(And on the earth are signs for those of certain faith) (20)
Pointing to the signs on earth, within the soul, and in the heaven:
This is connected to the signs mentioned above. The signs of the winds and clouds relate to the space between the heavens and earth. Now, He points to the signs in the heavens, on the earth, and within the human soul, starting first with the signs on the earth, then within the soul, and then in the heaven. Although the Qur'an has used them to prove all its fundamental claims—monotheism, Resurrection, and messengership—as detailed in the previous Surahs, keeping in view the core theme of this Surah, He points here specifically to the signs of the Resurrection and recompense. Therefore, we will also restrict our discussion to these, and content ourselves with brief references as the Qur'an did, because they have been discussed in detail.
First, reflect on the verses of Surah An-Naba, where Allah Almighty has argued for the Resurrection and recompense from these signs. He states:
(Have We not made the earth a resting place, and the mountains as pegs? And We created you in pairs, and made your sleep [a means for] rest, and made the night as clothing, and made the day for livelihood, and constructed above you seven strong [heavens], and made [therein] a burning lamp, and sent down from the rain clouds pouring water, that We may bring forth thereby grain and vegetation, and gardens of thick growth? Indeed, the Day of Judgement is an appointed time) (An-Naba, 78:6-17).
In these verses, referencing the signs of His power, mercy, and providence in the heavens, earth, and what is between them, Allah Almighty proved that this creation is not a vain plaything; therefore, a Day of Decision must inevitably come when He judges people. This was explained, and we briefly refer here to some of the earth's signs that prove the recompense:
Some signs on earth that prove the recompense:
- Regarding the possibility of the Resurrection, He argued from the earth's signs: you witness daily that the earth is completely dead and barren, with no trace of life, and then He sends His rain of mercy, which revives it and makes it lush with vegetation. Reflect: can the God Who displays this power daily find it difficult to resurrect people after they have turned to dust?
- Referencing the arrangement of providence He made for human sustenance, He asked: 'Will the Lord Who made this arrangement leave you unbridled, without bringing a Day to question those who used His blessings for rebellion and reward those who appreciated them?' Do you assume He is a plaything, in Whose sight the righteous and the wicked are equal, and do His blessings impose no responsibility on you?
- The Qur'an also points to the ruins of past nations on earth, showing that they were destroyed for their transgression and rebelliousness. He preserved their ruins so that subsequent generations would take warning, knowing that the Creator is not unconcerned with what occurs on earth; rather, He is watching everything, and when a nation's transgression exceeds the limit, it faces retribution. This historical law is a clear proof that a Day of universal justice will come, when every rebel is punished and every righteous person is rewarded.
These are some of the obvious signs on earth to which He has pointed, and which we have explained in detail in this book. Repeating them here would be lengthy.
When there is no desire to believe, proofs do not benefit:
'for those of certain faith' (lil-mooqineen) - That is: there is no lack of signs on earth; they are present at every corner, provided there are eyes to see, intellect to understand, and hearts to believe. The meaning is: accepting a reality does not depend solely on the presence of proofs, but requires that the listener possesses the intention to understand and accept. If he lacks this intention, he will always fabricate some excuse to reject the most obvious truth. History shows that denial of the truth did not occur because of a lack of proofs, but because the desire to believe was absent, which is an incurable disease.

(And within yourselves. Then will you not see?) (21)
Pointing to some of the internal proofs:
This points to the internal proofs of the Recompense. Just as He argued from nature, He argued from the human soul for all His fundamental claims, as we explain. Here, we briefly refer to some aspects regarding the Recompense:
- The Qur'an repeatedly draws attention to man's heedlessness: can the God Who turned an insignificant drop of water through various stages into a human being, equipping him with physical and spiritual capabilities, find it difficult to resurrect him after death to account for his deeds? Since the first creation was not impossible, why should the second be? He also explains that every human experiences a model of life, death, barzakh, and resurrection daily, provided he reflects on his experiences.
Man's status as representative requires presenting himself before God:
- Referencing the capabilities of intellect, expression, and choice He endowed within man, He explained: man is not like other animals, but holds a superior status—carrying the trust of choice and will, because of which He honored him with representation (khilafah) on earth. This representation requires that he must present himself before his Lord one Day, so that those who fulfilled this trust receive His eternal reward, and those who betrayed it face His punishment. Thus, recompense is the essential requirement of his representation on earth. This was explained, and Jesus also clarified it through excellent parables.
The existence of the self-accusing soul within man is a proof of the Resurrection:
- Third, as explained with special care in Surah Al-Qiyamah, He endowed man with a self-accusing soul (nafs-i-lawwamah) that corrects and blames him when he commits an evil. Swearing by this self-accusing soul, He presented it as a testimony to the Resurrection: if the Creator did not intend to reward goodness and punish evil, why did He place this constant friction within man? Its existence is a clear witness that He willed to place a small model of the Resurrection within every human heart, reminding him that the Creator is not unconcerned with his deeds, but is the One Who recompenses. This is why man is called a microcosm, as the reflection of the entire macrocosm is within him.
Surah Al-Qiyamah states that the testimony to the Recompense is present within every person, even if he presents excuses: (Rather, man, against himself, will be a witness, even if he presents his excuses) (75:14-15), and: (Rather, man wants to sin right in front of himself) (75:5)—meaning: the court of his conscience is within him; therefore, his crime is committed right in front of this court, which is a great audacity.
It should noted that the self-accusing soul continues to perform this duty of warning until the person completely deadens it through constant violation, as explained.
'Then will you not see?' (afala tubsiroon) - Since the soul is closest to man, He asked: 'Are even these close signs not visible to you!' meaning: if the corners of the earth are beyond your reach and the heaven is distant, is even your own soul distant that you cannot look within it!

(And in the heaven is your provision and whatever you are promised) (22)
Pointing to the signs of the heaven:
Now, He points to the signs of the heaven: in it is your provision and also the punishment of which you are warned. 'Provision' (rizq) refers to rain, which is the source of sustenance—using the cause to represent the effect, which is a common Arabic style. 'Whatever you are promised' (ma to'adoon) refers to the punishment of which they were warned if they rejected the Messenger, as explained under verse 5. Examples of this exist in history, and some will be mentioned further on in this Surah.
That is: this heaven displays the signs of recompense daily—sending rain for your sustenance from it, and pouring down punishment upon the rebellious from it whenever He wills. He needs no special preparation to send His mercy or His punishment; so how can you regard it as impossible that He turns the very rain that sustains your life into a source of your destruction! Furthermore, since you witness His mercy and wrath daily in this world, why do you not believe He is not unconcerned with good and evil, but will punish the wicked and reward the righteous.

(Then by the Lord of the heaven and the earth, indeed, it is the truth—just as that you are speaking) (23)
Summary of the discussion:
Now, He summarizes all these signs and, swearing by the Lord of the heavens and earth, states: the Day of Recompense of which you are warned and the consequence of rejecting the Messenger are certain. Allah will face no difficulty in this. Just as uttering a word is easy for you, performing this is easy for Him. His works are completed by His command 'Be'.
In 'Indeed, it' (innahu), the reference of the pronoun is the subject of the oaths mentioned above: (Indeed, what you are promised is true, and indeed, the Recompense is to occur). Since it was mentioned, the pronoun is sufficient. First He swore by the signs of the heavens and earth, and then He swore by the Lord of the heavens and earth to state the same point—showing that just as every product reflects the style of its craftsman, the signs of the heavens and earth reflect the attributes of their Creator: He is the One Who recompenses. If this were not so, where did this reflection appear from?
Explaining the occurrence of the Resurrection through an example:
'Indeed, it is the truth—just as that you are speaking' (innahu la-haqqum-mithla ma annakum tantiqoon) - 'Haqq' (truth) has two meanings: first, this Recompense is a solid reality in which there is no room for doubt; second, it is bound to happen, and no one should regard its occurrence as impossible. When the appointed time comes, He will bring it forth. Its being a reality was proved by the signs of nature and the human soul. Now, He explains its occurrence through an example: this task is not difficult for the Lord of the heavens and earth. Since He created the heavens and earth without facing any difficulty, why should resurrecting people for the account be difficult? Just as uttering a word is easy for you, performing any great task is easy for Him. He needs no special preparation, but performs every task through His command 'Be' in the twinkling of an eye. This is explained in various places in the Qur'an:
(Our only word to a thing when We intend it is that We say to it, 'Be,' and it is.) (An-Nahl, 16:40).
And in Surah Ya-Sin:
(Is not He Who created the heavens and the earth Able to create the likes of them? Yes, [He is], and He is the Knowing Creator. His command is only when He intends a thing that He says to it, 'Be,' and it is.) (Ya-Sin, 36:81-82).
Elsewhere, He explained that recreating this world is even easier than the first creation:
(And He is the One Who originates creation; then He repeats it, and that is [even] easier for Him) (Ar-Rum, 30:27).
Keep in mind that this comparison is merely a metaphor for our comprehension, whereas the reality is that even uttering a word is not as easy for us as creating the entire universe is for Him. We depend on various means to speak—which are granted by Him—whereas He is independent of everything. Elsewhere, He compared it to the twinkling of an eye: (And Our command is but one, like the twinkling of an eye) (Al-Qamar, 54:50). This is also a metaphor to convey His absolute authority.
A point from our teacher:
Our teacher explained the coherence among the signs of the earth, the soul, and the heaven, writing:
'Reflecting on this comprehensive discourse, there is an excellent coherence and a progressive order. First He mentioned the earth, then the soul, and then the heaven. Note that the soul exists between the earth and the heaven, containing both a physical and a spiritual aspect—inclining toward the earth from one aspect, and toward the heaven from the other. Having pointed to these signs, He swore by: "Then by the Lord of the heaven and the earth, indeed, it is the truth", which is the primary proof of the Resurrection—namely: His providence. Then He reinforced this through the metaphor of speaking, drawn from the qualities of the soul, which serves as a microcosm reflecting the heavens and earth, pointing back to: "And within yourselves. Then will you not see?"'

7. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (24-46)
Now, the Qur'an presents the historical testimonies in support of the claims mentioned above. It is His established law to present historical testimonies alongside the signs of nature and the human soul, so that the proof is established, and those who possess some capacity can learn a lesson. Reflecting on these accounts, three aspects are prominent:
- First, the winds' actions played a primary role in the destruction of these nations. Thus, these events confirm the oaths sworn at the beginning of the Surah.
- Second, both aspects of His recompense are prominent—namely, mercy and wrath. A single thing became a source of destruction for one, and a source of salvation for another.
- Third, His grasp is irresistible. No nation, no matter how powerful, can resist His power when He wills to destroy them. Read the verses.
8. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(Has there reached you the story of the honored guests of Abraham?) (24)
Historical accounts testifying to His mercy and wrath:
Above, under: 'And in the heaven is your provision and whatever you are promised', we noted that His power possesses both mercy and wrath. Rain from the heaven is a great mercy, but He turns it into a punishment when He wills.
To explain this, the historical accounts begin with the story of Abraham (peace be upon him) and Lot (peace be upon him)—showing that before the punishment befell the nation of Lot, the angels who brought His wrath first appeared to Abraham with the glad tiding of a knowledgeable son.
The address 'Has there reached you' (hal ataka) does not necessarily refer to the Prophet, but to those deniers on whom the proof is established. When a group is addressed through a singular pronoun, the objective is to focus on every individual within the group. The interrogative style also indicates that the subsequent matter is important, and everyone must listen to it with attention.
'Dayf' (guest) is used for both singular and plural; hence, its attribute here is 'mukrameen' (honored). 'Mukrameen' points to the respect, welcome, and hospitality Abraham showed them. This hospitality proves that their nobility was prominent from their appearance, and did not need to be explained. Thus, although they were strangers to him, he immediately began to prepare for their hospitality, arranging the best food in haste. But when they reached Lot (peace be upon him), his nation rose to humiliate them, and Lot had to defend their honor. Ultimately, the angels had to reveal their identity and destroy the nation.

(When they entered upon him and said, 'Peace.' He said, 'Peace, [you are] a people unknown') (25)
Abraham (peace be upon him) and his guests:
That is: they greeted Abraham with peace in the manner of the noble, and Abraham responded to them in like manner. This response cleared the initial strangeness and assured him of their nobility, but a question arose in his mind: Who are these people? Where do they come from? What is their objective? They are completely unknown to me.
He said 'a people unknown' (qawmum-munkaroon) in his heart, not verbally, because there was no reason to say it verbally. He felt surprised because in that region, there was a lack of noble and righteous individuals, and the few who existed belonged to his own associates; hence, he wondered where such noble people had emerged from in this desert.
Note that 'qala' (he said) is used for what is spoken by the tongue, and also for what is thought in the heart, as is common in classical Arabic and the Qur'an.

(So he slipped off to his family and brought a fat calf) (26)
'Ragh' (slipped off) means performing a task secretly to avoid attention. Abraham (peace be upon him), seeing them, slipped off to his family to arrange for their food, doing so secretly because he did not want his efforts to become a burden on his guests. This is the way of the noble, generous host—arranging for hospitality in a manner that does not make the guest feel uneasy.
'And brought a fat calf' (fa-ja'a bi-'ijlin sameen) - That is: he had a fat calf slaughtered and presented its roasted meat. 'A fat calf' (bijlin sameen) demonstrates his generosity—showing that he had a whole calf slaughtered to feed a few guests. This does not require that he presented the entire calf; occasionally, the whole is mentioned to represent a part.

(And placed it near them; he said, 'Will you not eat?') (27)
An anxiety of Abraham (peace be upon him):
This sentence contains an ellipsis understood from context. The meaning is: he placed the food near them, but seeing that they did not stretch their hands to eat, he invited them to eat with affection. In other places, the Qur'an has explicitly explained this ellipsis.

(And he felt from them a fear. They said, 'Fear not,' and gave him glad tidings of a knowledgeable boy) (28)
That is: when they did not stretch their hands to eat, the strangeness he had felt initially grew, and he felt a fear in his heart. Surah Hud indicates that this fear arose because they did not accept his hospitality: (But when he saw their hands not reaching to it, he felt them stranger and felt from them a fear) (Hud, 11:70).
It appears that their not eating made him realize they were not humans, but angels. That angels do not eat was well known, and could not be hidden from a prophet of Abraham's stature. Furthermore, he knew that when angels descend, they do so for a major mission. Also, the corruption of the nation of Lot next door had reached the limit; hence, they were ready for His punishment. Under these circumstances, he feared that the time of their destruction had arrived. The angels تاڑ (perceived) his anxiety and reassured him: 'Fear not,' and to reassure him further, they gave him the glad tiding of a knowledgeable boy. This was a great glad tiding because it was of a 'knowledgeable' boy, implying he would be blessed with prophethood. Abraham was reassured regarding himself, but his question regarding their mission remained, which he asked later.

(Then his wife approached with a cry and struck her face and said, '[I am] a barren old woman!') (29)
The reaction of Sarah to the glad tiding:
This glad tiding was delivered to Abraham (peace be upon him) aloud; hence, his wife, Sarah, who was standing nearby, heard it. The amazement and joy she felt can easily be imagined; thus, she approached in amazement, struck her face, and said: 'I am a barren old woman, how can I give birth at this age!' Her words express her amazement, joy, and desire for further confirmation.
'With a cry' (fi sarrah) - she approached in a state of amazement and surprise. In Arabic, 'sarra-l-farsu udhnayhi' means: the horse stood its ears. From this, 'fi sarrah' is used to express a state of amazement.
'And struck her face' (fa-sallat wajhaha) - This is how women express amazement. In these few words, the Qur'an has portrayed her amazement and joy.

(They said, 'Thus has said your Lord; indeed, He is the Wise, the Knowing') (30)
Reassurance from the angels:
The angels responded: 'Be assured. Thus has said your Lord, and when He has commanded, it will surely happen. Neither your being a barren old woman nor your husband's old age can stand as an obstacle. Allah is ever Wise and Knowing; His wisdom and knowledge encompass everything, and all causes are subject to His command.'

([Abraham] said, 'Then what is your business, O messengers?') (31)
Abraham's question and its nature:
When Abraham (peace be upon him) was reassured regarding himself and knew they were angels, he asked: 'O messengers! What is your business?' He asked because he knew that when angels descend in this manner, they do so for a major mission; if only the glad tiding was intended, this arrangement was not necessary. 'Khatb' (business) is used in Arabic for a major and important matter. Since he harbored anxiety regarding the nation of Lot, he wanted to clarify if that was their mission.

(They said, 'Indeed, we have been sent to a people of criminals') (32)
The angels' response:
The angels responded: 'We have been sent to a people of criminals.' It appears they did not name the nation of Lot here, but Surah Hud states: (They said, 'Fear not, indeed we have been sent to the people of Lot') (Hud, 11:70). Combining both, they mentioned both their character and name to show Abraham they deserved the punishment. But in this Surah, for the sake of eloquence, their name was omitted and they were described only as 'a people of criminals' to highlight that the punishment befell them because of their deeds, which is the core theme of this Surah.

(To send down upon them stones of clay, marked in the presence of your Lord for the transgressors) (33-34)
This states the purpose of their mission: to pour down stones of clay upon them. 'Ala' (upon) is a proof that they would be completely devastated by this. 
'Stones of clay' (hijaratam-min-teen) refers to pebbles of clay that have become like stones. In other places, the word 'sijjeel' (baked clay) is used—for example: (and We rained upon them stones of hard clay [sijjeel]) (Hud, 11:82). 'Sijjeel' is the arabized form of the Persian 'sang-i-gil' (baked clay), and 'hijaratam-min-teen' clarifies its meaning. 
'Musawwamah' means marked. In my view, it is a circumstantial clause derived from 'hijarah'—meaning: in Allah's plan, these stones are marked and reserved specifically for the wicked of the nation of Lot. Just as road construction materials are marked by workers to show they are reserved, similarly, these stones are reserved by His power, and no one should touch them. Surah Hud states: (And they [i.e., the stones] are not from the wrongdoers distant) (Hud, 11:83)—pointing to the reality that the very earth on which they walk contains these stones, which His wind will raise and pour upon them.
'For the transgressors' (lil-musrifeen) points to the wicked of the nation of Lot. 'Israf' means transgressing His limits, used for major and minor transgressions. Here, it refers to the indecency in which they were steeped. When a nation transgresses His limits, He unleashes His creation to punish them.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 33 and 34 was combined in the original text.]

(35-37)
Allah's conduct with the nation of Lot:
The angels' statement ended. Now, this describes Allah's conduct with them. It is stated: before sending the punishment, We brought out those who believed. In 'feeha' (therein), the reference is the settlements of the nation of Lot. Since these examples are under: 'And on the earth are signs for those of certain faith', the pronoun appeared without a prior explicit noun, which is grammatically accepted when there is context.
This points to the divine law: after His proof is established, He saves those who believed before the punishment. Regarding the nation of Lot, He directed them to depart from the settlements before dawn, without anyone looking back.
'But We did not find within it other than a [single] house of Muslims' (fa-ma wajadna feeha ghayra baytim-minal-muslimeen) - That is: We found only a single house of Muslims in that basty. This was indeed the house of Lot (peace be upon him), and from it, his wife was excluded because her support was with her nation.
The mention of the scarcity of believers highlights that Allah Almighty sends His decisive punishment when the entire society has become corrupt, and the believers are completely absent or so few that they are virtually non-existent. This was also indicated in Abraham's dispute, and is the requirement of His justice and mercy.
A point worthy of attention:
Note that the earlier verse used the word 'believers' (mu'mineen), and this verse uses 'Muslims' (muslimeen). This is because the earlier verse described His law of saving, and salvation belongs only to true believers. This verse describes the state of the basty, where only a single house of Muslims existed. For this house, He used 'Muslims', which is wider, encompassing all members, including those who were young or weak. Indeed, Lot's wife was also included initially, but was excluded at the end.
Some signs of the punishment on earth:
'And We left therein a sign for those who fear the painful punishment' (wa tarakna feeha ayatan-lilladhina yakhafoona-l-'adhaba-l-aleem) - That is: We left a prominent sign in that basty for those who wish to take warning from His wrath on earth. Recall the verse: (And on the earth are signs for those of certain faith). This is a testimony drawn from the ruins of the earth which the Quraysh frequently passed by. We explained under Surah Al-Hijr that the settlements of Lot—Sodom and Gomorrah—were located on the trade route between Hijaz and Syria. The meaning is: they are witnessing these ruins, yet they are denying; so they will believe only when the storm of punishment befalls their own heads.
The nature of the stone-showering upon the nation of Lot:
Now, understand the nature of the stone-showering mentioned in verse 33, to show its relationship with the oaths of the winds and clouds.
Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) explained its nature:
'Regarding the destruction of the nation of Lot, He unleashed a violent wind that became a stone-showering wind, pelting them with stones and overturning their homes. He stated: (And [We destroyed] the ones of the overturned cities whom He overthrew, and covered them with what He covered) (An-Najm, 53:53-54) and: (So We made the highest part [of the city] its lowest and rained upon them stones of hard clay) (Al-Hijr, 15:74)—meaning a violent wind destroyed their homes, and covered them with stones and sand, as He stated: (So each We seized for his sin; and among them were those upon whom We sent a storm of stones) (Al-Ankabut, 29:40).'
'Furthermore, if we combine this with the Torah, it shows they faced both the stone-showering wind and thunder and lightning.'
Thus, the account of Lot contains the testimony of both oaths—namely, the violent wind and the winter clouds.
This was also discussed in Surah Hud and Surah Al-Hijr; refer to them for details.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 35, 36, and 37 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 35, 36, and 37 was combined in the original text.]

(And in Moses, when We sent him to Pharaoh with a clear authority) (38)
Reference to the account of Moses (peace be upon him) and Pharaoh:
This is connected to the account of Abraham. That is: just as Abraham's account contains His nusr (help), justice, and retribution, similarly Moses' account contains them.
The analysis of 'sultan' (authority) was presented: it means clear proof, and also awe and status. Here, it encompasses both. The signs Allah granted Moses were clear proofs. Pharaoh called them magic out of pride, though he knew they were miracles. These miracles established such an awe of Moses in Pharaoh's heart that he could not dare to attack him until the end.

(But he turned away with his support and said, '[He is] a magician or madman') (39)
'Rukn' means shoulder, and 'bi' (with) here implies transgression. When a person turns away from something with pride, he does so with a movement of his shoulders; hence, it means: he turned away with pride. This style is common in the Qur'an—for example:
(And when We bestow favor upon man, he turns away and distances himself) (Al-Isra, 17:83),
and in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 9, this is described as: 'turning his cheek' (thaniya 'itfihi). 'And said, "[He is] a magician or madman"' (wa qala sahirun aw majnoon) - That is: sometimes they called him a magician, and sometimes mad. Seeing his miracles, they called him a magician; and hearing his call, they called him mad, claiming he was calling to a God whom no one had seen.

(So We seized him and his hosts and cast them into the sea, while he was blameworthy) (40)
That is: We seized him and his hosts—which were his source of pride and caused his transgression—and cast them into the sea. Their drowning is described in the previous Surahs, and here He states: 'We cast them into the sea', as if their existence was like a handful of dust. This is an expression of His absolute authority—showing that the greatest power of the arrogant is like worthless straw before His power.
'while he was blameworthy' (wa huwa muleem) - That is: he was responsible for his end, and no one else could be blamed. Allah Almighty clarified the truth to him through His Messenger, but pride blinded his intellect, and he led his entire nation to destruction.
While the details are not mentioned here, other places in the Qur'an and the Torah clarify that His winds played a primary role in their destruction. Our teacher explained this:
The nature of the destruction of Pharaoh and his hosts:
'Regarding this, the Torah's Book of Exodus states:
"Then Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and all that night the Lord drove the sea back with a strong east wind and turned it into dry land. The waters were divided." (Exodus 14:21).
"This wind blew all night and stopped in the morning. Its force drove the water to the west in the Gulf of Suez, leaving the eastern gulf dry. When the wind stopped, the water returned, and Pharaoh's army was drowned." This is confirmed by the Qur'an, in Surah Ad-Dukhan:
(Travel with My servants by night. Indeed, you will be pursued. And leave the sea at rest [rahwan]. Indeed, they are an army to be drowned.) (Ad-Dukhan, 44:23-24).
"Rahwan" means at rest, which is achieved when the wind stops. In Surah Ta-Ha:
(And We inspired to Moses, "Travel by night with My servants and strike for them a dry path through the sea; you will not fear being overtaken [by Pharaoh] nor be afraid [of drowning]." So Pharaoh pursued them with his soldiers, and there covered them from the sea that which covered them.) (Ta-Ha, 20:77-78).
In Exodus 15:10, Moses' song of praise is:
"You blew with Your wind, the sea covered them."
In Deuteronomy 11:4:
"and what He did to the Egyptian army, to its horses and chariots, how He overwhelmed them with the waters of the Red Sea as they were pursuing you, and how the Lord brought ruin on them to this day."
"The summary is: Allah Almighty saved Moses through the violent wind, and destroyed Pharaoh through the gentle wind—showing both aspects of His mercy and wrath through the winds."' 
Thus, the account of Moses and Pharaoh is an excellent example of His power and recompense, and is among the signs on earth pointed to in: (And on the earth are signs for those of certain faith).

(And in Aad, when We sent upon them the barren wind) (41)
Reference to the account of Aad:
This points to the end of Aad—showing that their account contains a great lesson: We sent upon them the barren wind (Al-Reeh-al-'aqeem). 'Aqeeem' refers to the wind that is barren, bringing no rain or benefit. In Arabic, rain-bringing winds are called 'Al-Waqih' (those that fecundate), and barren winds are 'aqeem'. It refers to the cold, dry winter wind, as stated in Surah Fussilat:
(So We sent upon them a screaming wind in days of misfortune) (Fussilat, 41:16).

(It left nothing of what it came upon but that it made it like decayed ruins) (42)
This portrays the destructive impact of this wind: it turned everything it passed into decayed ruins. 'Ramem' refers to decayed wood, rope, or bones. The cold, dry wind deprives everything of life and strength, and being violent, it destroys and pulverizes crops, vegetation, and all living things. This was expressed in Surah Al-Qamar as: (Indeed, We sent upon them a screaming wind on a day of continuous misfortune, extricating people as if they were uprooted trunks of palm trees) (54:19-20).

(And in Thamud, when it was said to them, 'Enjoy yourselves for a time.' But they were insolent toward the command of their Lord, so the thunderbolt seized them while they were looking on) (43-44)
Reference to the account of Thamud:
Following Aad, this points to the end of Thamud, showing that their account contains a great lesson. 'when it was said to them, "Enjoy yourselves for a time"' (idh qeela lahum tamatta'oo hatta heen) refers specifically to the time when their leader hamstrung the she-camel and Salih warned them: 'Enjoy yourselves in your homes for a little; the time of your destruction has arrived.' What is stated briefly here is detailed in Surah Hud as: (But they hamstrung her. So he said, 'Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days. That is a promise not to be denied.') (Hud, 11:65)—showing that they were granted three days' respite to repent and save themselves from the punishment.
'But they were insolent toward the command of their Lord, so the thunderbolt seized them while they were looking on' (fa-'ataw 'an amri rabbihim fa-akhadhat-humu-s-sa'iqatu wa hum yandhuroon) - 'Atow' means pride and disobedience, and connected with 'an', it implies turning away. The meaning is: they ignored this final warning and turned away from His command; consequently, He seized them with the thunderbolt while they were looking on.
'Sa'iqah' means a shout or cry, referring to the punishment that befell them. Surah Hud used 'sayhah' (the shout). How this appeared will be clarified.
'while they were looking on' (wa hum yandhuroon) implies several things:
First, the punishment came openly, and they witnessed it but could not protect themselves.
Second, it came suddenly, leaving no opportunity to escape, as stated: (Indeed, We sent upon them a single shout, and they became like the dry twig-fragments of an [animal] pen-builder) (Al-Qamar, 54:31).
Third, seeing it, they were struck with panic, which is explained next.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43 and 44 was combined in the original text.]

(And they were unable to stand, nor were they helped [i.e., defended]) (45)
That is: hearing the shout, they were struck with panic and could not stand, but fell prostrate. Surah Al-A'raf described their state as: (So the earthquake seized them, and they became within their home [corpses] fallen prone) (Al-A'raf, 7:91).
'Nor were they helped' (wa ma kanu muntasireen) - 'Intisar' means defending oneself. That is: they could not defend themselves against Allah's punishment. A classical poet wrote:
'The dog dug its claws into the gazelle's thigh; so I said to it, "Defend yourself [ala tantasir]!"'
Now, understand the nature of the punishment of Aad and Thamud, to clarify its relationship with the oaths of the winds and clouds. Our teacher explained this:
'Reflecting on the Qur'an's description of Aad's destruction, the violent wind was accompanied by winter clouds that appeared with thunder and lightning. Where their destruction is mentioned, the wind is accompanied by clouds and thunderbolts. In Surah Al-Ahqaf, He states:
(And when they saw it as a cloud approaching their valleys, they said, "This is a cloud bringing us rain." Rather, it is that which you were impatient for—a wind, within it a painful punishment, destroying everything by command of its Lord.) (Al-Ahqaf, 46:24-25).
"Obviously, these are the characteristics of winter clouds and winds, when the violent northern wind blows, bringing قحط (drought) and destruction."'
'Similarly, in Surah Fussilat, the thunderbolt is mentioned regarding Aad: (But if they turn away, then say, "I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of Aad and Thamud") (Fussilat, 41:13).'
'This shows that Allah Almighty sent upon them the wind, clouds, and thunderbolts, but their destruction was caused primarily by the wind; therefore, if we argue from the effect, we learn that He sent upon Thamud the winter clouds containing thunderbolts, just as He sent upon Aad the wind and clouds. Since Thamud was destroyed by the thunderbolt, only it was mentioned; but the presence of clouds is implied. Similarly, regarding Aad, the wind is mentioned repeatedly, and the cloud is mentioned in only one place.'

(And [We destroyed] the people of Noah before. Indeed, they were a people defiantly disobedient) (46)
Reference to the account of the nation of Noah:
This is connected to the preceding accounts—meaning: We destroyed the nation of Noah before them in like manner. Connecting to the meaning of the preceding verses is common in the Qur'an.
Finally, reference is made briefly to the account of the nation of Noah. Chronologically, this was the first event, but the Qur'an has not adopted chronological order here, because it directed the Quraysh first to the events whose ruins were present in their country and pointed to in: (And on the earth are signs for those of certain faith). This required mentioning the nation of Lot and the people of Pharaoh first, as they were closer to them in space and time, and could make a greater impact. Finally, reference is made to the first event to complete the history.
Note also that like the other accounts where His winds played a primary role, the nation of Noah was destroyed through His wind. Our teacher explained this:
'The Qur'an and the Torah indicate that His winds played a primary role in the destruction of the nation of Noah. In Surah Al-Ankabut, He states:
(And We certainly sent Noah to his people, and he remained among them a thousand years save fifty years, and the flood [At-Tufan] seized them while they were wrongdoers) (Al-Ankabut, 29:14).'
'"Tufan" literally means rotating and spinning. The Arabs use it for a violent wind that rises by spinning rapidly.'
'In other languages as well, similar words exist—such as "gard-bad" (whirlwind) in Persian, "cyclone" in English, and "typhoon" (which was the name of a wind deity) in Egyptian. This wind causes heavy rain and makes the sea rage. I witnessed such a storm in Karachi: it rose from the east in the Indian Ocean, causing heavy rain, smashing ships against mountains, and causing immense loss. The details of Noah's flood in the Torah and the Qur'an are similar to this. In Surah Al-Qamar:
(So We opened the gates of heaven with pouring rain, and caused the earth to burst with springs, and the waters met for a matter already predestined) (Al-Qamar, 54:11-12).'
'And in Genesis 7:11:
"all the springs of the great deep burst forth, and the floodgates of the heavens were opened."'
'In Surah Hud:
(And it sailed with them through waves like mountains) (Hud, 11:42).
Waves rising like mountains occurs when a violent wind blows.'
'He summarized:
"Indeed, the nation of Noah faced a storm of violent wind, causing heavy rain and making the seas rage. In this storm, Noah's ark came to rest on Mount Judi."'
9. Coherence of the Accounts:
While we have explained the wisdom behind their order, our teacher has written a chapter on this, explaining some subtle points:
'The account of Abraham and Lot contains both a glad tiding and a warning. Note that this is the exact characteristic of the winds sworn by in the introduction—sometimes appearing as a mercy, and sometimes as a punishment. This comprehensive nature of Abraham's account made it suitable to serve as the introduction.'
'Next, the account of the nation of Lot is narrated because the Quraysh frequently passed by their ruins. Furthermore, it has a close relationship with the first part of the oaths: "By those [winds] that scatter scattering, and those [clouds] that carry a load"—showing that their destruction was by the wind that pelted them with stones and sand, covering their settlements.'
Furthermore, the claim: (And on the earth are signs for those of certain faith) is proved by the ruins of Lot's basty.
'Next is the account of Moses, which is narrated repeatedly in the Qur'an and contains great lessons. It has a close relationship with the second part of the oaths: "and those [clouds] that carry a load, and those [ships] that glide with ease" [implying the gentle wind that saved them and drowned Pharaoh], which has been explained.'
'Note also that the accounts beginning with the names of the prophets highlight the aspect of mercy and glad tidings, while those beginning with the names of the nations highlight the aspect of warning. Among the nations, Aad and Thamud are mentioned specifically because they were destroyed by the striped clouds (wa-s-sama'i dhat-il-hubuk). The order of the oaths is maintained in the order of these accounts. Aad is mentioned before Thamud because of chronological precedence, and also because they were destroyed by the wind and clouds together.'
'The account of Noah is an eternal sign of Allah's mercy for all nations, as He stated:
(Indeed, when the water overflowed, We carried you in the sailing vessel, that We might make it for you a reminder and [that] a conscious ear would be conscious of it) (Al-Haqqah, 69:11-12).'
'This account combines the wonders of the earth, heaven, wind, clouds, ark, and water; therefore, it serves as a comprehensive assembly of proofs. Thus, after the signs of nature, the soul, and the heaven were mentioned, it was appropriate to present this comprehensive account to depict the reality.'
'Furthermore, Aad and Thamud succeeded the nation of Noah; therefore, referencing Noah's account was appropriate, as He stated in Surah An-Najm:
(And that He destroyed the first Aad, and Thamud, and He did not spare [them], and the people of Noah before. Indeed, they were more unjust and oppressive.) (An-Najm, 53:50-52).'
'Note also that He used "the people of Noah" (wa qawma Nuh) instead of "and in Noah" (wa fi Nuh) to highlight its unique status and distinguish it from the other accounts.'

10. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (47-60)
Next, drawing attention to His power and providence, Allah Almighty warns of the Resurrection and recompense, and reminds of monotheism—showing that everyone must face Him alone, and no one will save them from His grasp. Finally, He comforts the Prophet: 'Every nation treated their messenger in this manner, so continue in your work with patience, ignore the wicked, and remind only those who listen; Allah will help you and your companions, and you do not depend on their support. Tell those who are hastening the punishment that they should not be hasty; when their specified term is complete, the punishment will befall them, and it will be severe.'
11. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(And the heaven We constructed with strength, and indeed, We are [its] expander) (47)
Pointing to some of His signs of power:
'Ayd' means hands, and is used metaphorically for power and strength. The indefinite form highlights His majesty. 'Moose'oon' means: His authority and power are vast, and no great task is beyond His power.
Connected to the historical proofs, He draws attention to His power and majesty manifest in the vast heaven above. The meaning is: can the God Who created this vast heaven find it difficult to resurrect man after death? This appeared elsewhere as: (Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it) (An-Naziat, 79:27).
'And indeed, We are [its] expander' (wa inna la-moose'oon) - That is: do not assume Our entire power was exhausted in creating this heaven. Our power is limitless, and We can do whatever We will, whenever We will. This is expressed elsewhere as: (And We did not touch [i.e., suffer] any fatigue) (Qaf, 50:38).

(And the earth We have spread, and excellent is the preparer) (48)
Pointing to the earth after the heaven:
After the heaven, He directed them to the earth: look at how We have spread it, and how excellent We are in preparing it. That is: reflect on the earth, for it manifests Our power, majesty, and providence. Any reasonable person will reach the conclusion that the Creator Who made this earth and provided for human sustenance in it did not do so in vain. It is contrary to His wisdom to perform a vain work. Rather, His wisdom and providence require Him to bring a Day when every person is questioned whether he lived in accordance with His pleasure or chose his own way; then, the grateful will be rewarded, and the rebellious will be punished. This has been explained, and will be further discussed under Surah An-Naba.
'And excellent is the preparer' (fa-ni'ma-l-mahidon) points to the wonders of His wisdom and providence—showing that the Creator is not only powerful, but His wisdom, mercy, providence, and generosity are limitless. This requires that He bring a Day to judge His creation with perfect justice and mercy. If He did not, His mercy and providence would be negated, whereas every corner of the universe bears witness to them.

(And of all things We created two mates; perhaps you will remember) (49)
This draws attention to another aspect of the universe, which also serves as a proof of the Resurrection and recompense.
A specific aspect of the universe:
Allah has created everything in pairs in this world. The heaven and earth were mentioned. Every pair is completed only by joining its pair, which is a proof of monotheism—showing that a Wise and All-Powerful Creator has created this universe, establishing harmony among its diverse parts to make them purposeful. This is also a proof of the Resurrection, because this world, as a whole, has its pair—namely, the Hereafter—without which this world remains incomplete and vain. Believing in the Hereafter makes this world a wise work, and denying it makes it a vain plaything. This is why He asked those who deny it:
(Then did you think that We created you uselessly and that to Us you would not be returned?) (Al-Mu'minun, 23:115).
Here, 'perhaps you will remember' (la'allakum tadhakkaroon) shows that the pairing of everything in this world is a reminder that this world also has its pair—namely, the Hereafter.

(So flee to Allah. Indeed, I am to you from Him a clear warner) (50)
Reminding of the Hereafter:
That is: since the Hereafter is a solid reality, abandon your opposition to the Messenger, flee to your Lord, and prepare for the Day when everyone is questioned, and no one besides God will save you.
'Indeed, I am to you from Him a clear warner' (innee lakum-minhu nadheerum-mubeen) - That is: I have come to warn you clearly before the Day arrives, so that no one can claim they were not informed. I have warned you of its terrors, and now the responsibility lies on you.
The warning implied in 'a clear warner' was explained. 'From Him' (minhu) means: I have been sent by Him specifically for this mission of warning. Some have interpreted 'min' in connection with 'warner', but this is grammatically incorrect.

(And do not make [i.e., associate] with Allah another deity. Indeed, I am to you from Him a clear warner) (51)
Recalling that God has no partner:
This is not a repetition of the preceding verse, but explains another reality: do not assume that on that Day, your partners and intercessors will save you from God's grasp. Do not rely on such false supports, for on that Day, you will face only Allah, the One without partners. I have been commissioned to warn you clearly of this as well.
We explain that the polytheists regarded the Resurrection as unlikely, and even if they believed in it, they assumed their partners would save them. This delusion made the Resurrection a meaningless thing to them. To refute this, He mentioned monotheism alongside the Resurrection. Similar warnings appeared previously.

(Similarly, there came not to those before them any messenger but they said, '[He is] a magician or a madman') (52)
This comforts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'Do not grieve over their attitude. This is not specific to you, but the past nations treated their messengers in this exact manner.'
The attitude of the wicked toward the messengers:
'They said, "[He is] a magician or a madman"' (qalu sahirun aw majnoon) - That is: when they showed them miracles, they called them magicians; and when they warned them of the Resurrection, they called them mad, claiming they were calling to an unseen God. These words are mentioned as examples—showing that they fabricated excuses to deny the messengers, just as your people are doing. This has always been their way, and does not reflect any shortcoming in your efforts; so do not be disheartened, but be patient as the messengers of resolve were before you.

(Did they suggest it to them? Rather, they [themselves] are a transgressing people) (53)
This expresses amazement at the uniform attitude of the wicked throughout history: 'Did they suggest it to one another as a legacy?' as if every generation of the wicked left a will for the subsequent generation: 'If a messenger comes, treat him exactly as we did,' and every generation executed this will with loyalty.
'Rather, they [themselves] are a transgressing people' (bal hum qawmun taghoon) - This reveals the actual cause: they are also (the Quraysh) a transgressing people, just like the past deniers. This shared temperament caused them to choose the same crooked path, and they will inevitably face the same end.

(So turn away from them, for you are not to be blamed) (54)
Instruction to ignore the arrogant and focus on the good:
That is: ignore those wicked who are determined to oppose you. You have fulfilled your responsibility of communication, and are absolved before Allah; no questioning regarding them will lie on you. They are responsible for their actions, and will face the end.

(And remind, for indeed, the reminder benefits the believers) (55)
That is: ignore the arrogant leaders, but continue to remind those who listen. Those who seek faith will benefit from your reminder. This shows that the instruction to ignore relates specifically to those leaders of the Quraysh whose pride prevented them from listening. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was dedicated to bringing them to faith because he hoped this would strengthen Islam and open the path for others, but when the proof was established and it became clear that they were not going to believe, Allah Almighty stopped him from wasting his time on them.

(And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me. I do not want from them any provision, nor do I want them to feed Me. Indeed, it is Allah Who is the Provider, Firm in Strength) (56-58)
Stating two important realities:
Having commanded him to ignore the leaders of the Quraysh, He points to two important realities:
First, Allah did not create the jinn and mankind because of any need of His, such that He should please them. His sovereignty is established on His own might; He is independent of everyone, and everyone is in need of Him. He created them solely to fulfill the right of His worship, which is the way to reach His pleasure. He sent His messengers to remind them of this, and the Messenger has fulfilled his duty; so if they reject it, they destroy themselves. They harm neither Allah nor the Messenger, and there is no need to please them.
Thus, the reason for instructing the Prophet to ignore them is explained: 'His work will not stop without these arrogant leaders, so do not exhaust yourself over them.'
Second, the call of Allah's religion brings its own support. Those who stand to deliver this call should rely entirely on their Lord. They should remember that Allah created them to worship Him, which is the purpose of their life. For this purpose, they can sacrifice everything, but cannot sacrifice it for anything else. If they fear that their enemies will block their livelihood, they should stand firm and be assured: if they do not deviate from their purpose, Allah Almighty will open for them sustenance from where they least expect. Allah alone is the Provider, Firm in Strength, and no power can overcome His power.
This comforts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions (may Allah be pleased with them) at a time when the Quraysh were planning to expel them and block their livelihood: stand firm on the purpose of His worship for which your Lord created you. Allah alone is the Provider, and He is powerful over completing His responsibility. The servant's duty is worship; providing sustenance is Allah's responsibility, and He is not unable to complete His responsibility. This is expressed in other places where the Prophet is commanded to stand firm, ignoring the Quraysh:
(And enjoin prayer upon your family and be steadfast therein. We do not ask you for provision; We provide for you, and the [best] outcome is for righteousness.) (Ta-Ha, 20:132),
and in slightly different words:
(Do not extend your eyes toward that by which We have given enjoyment to [some] classes of them, and do not grieve over them. And lower your wing to the believers.) (Al-Hijr, 15:88).
Now, reflect on the parts of the discourse:
'And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me' (wa ma khalaqtu-l-jinna wa-l-insa illa liyabu'doon) - 'Worship' is used here in its wide sense: worshipping Him and obeying His commands. Stating this clear purpose is to keep the true objective of life foremost so that every human knows what to live for and what to die for. Note that His worship is not demanded because He is in need of it, but because the servant's maturity depends on it; without it, he is worse than animals.
'I do not want from them any provision, nor do I want them to feed Me' (ma ureedu minhum-mir-rizqin-wa ma ureedu ay-yut'imoon) - This is to clarify that Allah did not create them because of any need, as He is independent of everyone. He has not placed the responsibility of providing sustenance on His creation, nor does He eat or drink. This also deals a blow to the polytheists' delusion that their deities benefit from their offerings.
'Indeed, it is Allah Who is the Provider, Firm in Strength' (inna-llaha huwa-r-razzaqu dhu-l-quwwati-l-mateen) - No one should doubt His providence because of unfavorable circumstances. Allah alone is the Provider, and He is Firm in Strength, and no power of the enemy can defeat His planning.
Our teacher explained 'dhu-l-quwwati-l-mateen', writing:
'"Mateen" is used here for a strong, thick rope, and the strength of His power is described by it. While "quwwah" (power/strength) is feminine and "mateen" is masculine, the weight of "mateen" is identical for both masculine and feminine, as in: (Indeed, the mercy of Allah is near [qareeb] to the doers of good) (Al-A'raf, 7:56).'

(And indeed, for those who have wronged is a portion like the portion of their companions, so let them not hasten Me) (59)
Warning to the Quraysh:
'Dhanub' refers to a full bucket. It is used metaphorically for a person's portion or share. A classical poet wrote:
'By your life, there is no escape from death; for every father's sons, there is a portion [dhanub] in it.'
In the verse, 'dhanub' refers to the specified term of respite granted to these disbelievers. It is stated: just as He granted their associates (Aad, Thamud, etc.) a specified term of respite to fulfill their portion of sins, similarly He has granted them a term of respite to establish the proof. When this term is complete, the end of which they are warned will surely face them; so they should not hasten Him.

(And woe to those who disbelieved from their Day which they are promised) (60)
Although general, this refers specifically to the polytheists of the Quraysh who mocked the Messenger's warning, asking: 'If this punishment is to come, bring it immediately.' It is stated: let them not hasten this Day, for it will be extremely difficult, and no one will save them from the eternal destruction they will face on that Day.
With the help of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. All praise belongs to Him.
